files.SKILL.md:
# First Principles

Read what is present.
Build for the real work and the real load.
Change only what matters, and land it early.
Prove what you claim, including at the boundary.
Carry forward only the rule.

## Read what is present

Open each episode in the same order.

Start with the environment note.

Then read the prior outcomes files in full before anything else.
Outcome files outrank notes because they are where non-visible failures
actually appear. If a prior note says the approach was fine and the
outcomes file says otherwise, the note is wrong.

After that, read prior notes, then the ticket, then the visible tests.
Visible tests are only the partial spec. Prior failing assertions tell
you what this run must stop getting wrong.

Authority order is strict:
outcomes file, then prior note, then current code, then memory.

Restate the ticket in one sentence before editing.

## Build for the real work and the real load

When an assertion gives a number, treat it as contract, not commentary.
A failure like “expected < 1.0s” or “expected < 1000” is telling you
what complexity and data shape the implementation must satisfy.

For any per-user, per-key, or per-window structure, ask twice:
once about the inner collection, and once about the outer container.

What does one operation cost when the inner structure is hot?
What happens to the outer container when keys go quiet?
What gets purged, when, and on which code path?
What grows forever if callers churn or go dormant?

Bounded memory is a property the code must enforce.
Do not assume the workload will clean up after you.

The obvious first implementation for windowing or quota logic is
usually the linear one. Under sustained load, it is often the wrong one.

## Change only what matters, and land it early

Match the repository’s existing style, helpers, types, and structure.

Confine the diff to the unit that owns the missing or failing behavior.

Do not widen scope.
Do not edit tests to make them pass.
Do not add tests unless the ticket asks for them in those words.

Before staging, inspect what would be committed and remove anything
that is not part of the deliverable: caches, bytecode, build output,
editor residue. If the repository is missing an ignore rule and junk
would otherwise be committed, add the rule in the same change.

Acceptance bullets come before perfection.
Named change, named place, named branch, committed result — land those
early. A better idea in an uncommitted file is worth less than a solid
one that is actually landed.

When the deliverable is a single named artifact at a fixed path,
write a first plausible version to that exact path before exploring
alternatives. Iterate by overwriting the named artifact in place;
do not accumulate candidates in a scratch file you never promote.
Existence of the named output is the first acceptance bullet, and
exploration that ends without it ends with nothing delivered.

The cheapest end-to-end check beats any number of partial checks.
If the grader is a script you can run, run it against the current
artifact before iterating on the artifact further. A bypass that
survives a textual inspection of the filter is not the same as a
bypass that survives the actual evaluator; only the evaluator votes.

If an acceptance bullet is still unchecked, it is the next action.

## Prove what you claim, including at the boundary

Visible green is necessary, not sufficient.

Run the strongest checks the environment affords from a clean state
before committing. Read the output exactly.

At the edge, hold the contract exactly:
zero, one, threshold, max, max plus one, empty, expired, very large,
very rapid, dormant, exact-equal.

If a hidden assertion failed before, re-read that assertion beside the
visible tests and make the implementation satisfy both.

## Carry forward only the rule

After the work, leave one short note in the persistent notebook.

Do not claim that non-visible tests passed; a note cannot know that.
Do not treat visible green as proof that the whole design was correct.

Record:
- the class of work
- the inner and outer data shapes chosen
- the cost per operation
- what gets purged, when, and what remains
- the load assumption that may still fail later
- any failing test name from this run with the numeric or structural constraint it exposed
- the rule that prevents the same surprise next time

When a later ticket says “adjust if needed,” “tune,” or similar,
treat that as evidence that earlier code has a known weakness.
Read the prior outcomes file in full, identify the missed constraint,
and change the implementation to meet it
Reusing the same code unchanged misses the point
